title: fix(build): detect Bun runtime in build scripts

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces support for the Bun runtime and package manager across the project's build infrastructure, including pre-commit hooks and build scripts. It adds a detection mechanism for Bun and optimizes the build process by handling workspace dependencies and parallelism specifically for the Bun environment. Review feedback recommends using double quotes for shell arguments to maintain cross-platform compatibility on Windows and suggests that the Bun build logic should respect the CI environment variable by defaulting to sequential builds in CI, consistent with the existing npm implementation.

commit.message:
fix(build): detect Bun runtime in build scripts to avoid hardcoded npm

scripts/build.js, scripts/build_package.js, and the husky pre-commit
hook all invoke `npm` unconditionally. This breaks `bun install` and
committing on Bun-only systems where npm is not on PATH, since the
prepare script chain runs scripts/build.js and the pre-commit hook
runs on every commit.

Detect the runtime so the equivalent bun command is used when bun is
available and npm is not. The npm code paths are unchanged for npm
users:

- scripts/build.js: detect via `'bun' in process.versions` and use
  `bun install` / `bun run generate` / `bun run --filter '*' build`
  under Bun. The existing `npm` + `npm-run-all` parallel path is
  untouched.
- scripts/build_package.js: same `process.versions.bun` check for the
  chrome devtools MCP bundle step in @google/gemini-cli-core.
- .husky/pre-commit: detect via `command -v npm`; npm users keep their
  exact existing behaviour, bun-only systems fall through to `bun run
  pre-commit`.

Note: the root package.json `bundle` script still uses npm-style
workspace flags (`-w`, `--workspace=`) that aren't bun-compatible, so
`bun install` still fails further downstream of this fix; that's
tracked separately and out of scope here.

Fixes #26279

fix(build): mirror npm path's core-first ordering on Bun branch

Address PR review: bun run --filter does not guarantee topological
ordering for scripts, so building all workspaces in parallel risks
race conditions where dependents of @google/gemini-cli-core start
building before core is ready.

Mirror the npm branch's structure: build core first, then build the
remaining workspaces in parallel via --filter '*' --filter '!core'.
Drop the misleading comment claiming --filter handles ordering.

fix(build): use npm_config_user_agent for Bun detection; add CI sequential path

Address PR review:

- `'bun' in process.versions` only catches direct invocation (bun
  scripts/build.js). When invoked via `bun run build`, package.json
  routes through `node scripts/build.js`, so the runtime is Node and
  the check fails. Use `npm_config_user_agent` (set by the package
  manager and inherited by child processes) as a fallback. Both files
  apply the same detection.
- Mirror the npm path's CI handling on the Bun branch: in CI, build
  workspaces sequentially via `--sequential` to avoid resource
  exhaustion. Outside CI, build core first then build the rest in
  parallel via `--parallel`. The non-Bun npm path is unchanged.

fix(build): build core first under Bun in both CI and non-CI paths

bun run --filter does not respect workspace topological order — even
with --sequential, builds run in alphabetical/insertion order, which
means dependents of @google/gemini-cli-core can run before core. Lift
the core-first build out of the non-CI branch so both CI and non-CI
get the explicit ordering, matching the npm path's behavior.

fix(build): drop separate CI branch for Bun; always parallel after core

The Bun branch previously had separate CI and non-CI paths. The CI
path used --sequential, which review feedback objected to. Empirically
--sequential is a valid bun flag (listed in bun run --help and
producing serial Foreman-style output), but having two branches under
isBun is unnecessary complexity: bun's concurrent execution is fast
and well-behaved in CI, so collapse both paths to the same flow —
core first, then everything else in parallel.

The non-Bun npm path keeps its CI branch (which uses npm's own
sequential workspace execution).

commit.message:
fix(core): make shell tool work under Bun

Two related runtime issues prevented `bun start`/`bun`-launched builds of
gemini-cli from running shell tool calls:

1. The pty path silently captures no output under Bun — node-pty's
   `tty.ReadStream` wrapper around the master fd doesn't deliver data to
   `onData`, so the shell tool reports success with an empty result.
2. When `resizePty` does run, it hits `ioctl(2) failed, EBADF` because
   `destroyPtyProcess()` in `onExit` closes the master fd before the entry
   is removed from `activePtys`. The window is narrow on Node but widens
   under Bun's microtask scheduling, crashing the React render.

getPty() now short-circuits to the existing `child_process` fallback when
running under Bun (which already has Bun-specific `detached:false`
handling). `GEMINI_PTY_INFO=node-pty` overrides for testing.

resizePty() also swallows the `EBADF`/`ENOTTY` ioctl errors the same way
it already swallows `ESRCH` and the Windows "already exited" message —
they all mean the same thing: the pty is gone, nothing to resize.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat: implicitly use current repo and branch context for pr commands

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request enhances the CLI by allowing repository and branch context to be inferred from the local environment, making the `--repo` flag optional and enabling the `view` command to default to the current branch's pull request. The review feedback highlights several areas for improvement: refactoring duplicated repository inference logic, making the remote name configurable instead of hardcoding `origin`, increasing the robustness of the git URL parsing logic, and handling potential ambiguities when searching for pull requests in cross-repository setups.

commit.message:
feat: implicitly use current repo and branch context for pr commands

- Add util helpers CurrentRepo and CurrentBranch to parse local git context
- Remove required --repo flag from all pr subcommands, falling back to CurrentRepo
- Modify pr view to accept 0 args, inferring target PR via CurrentBranch and head matching
- Update api client ListPRs to support head filtering

review_comments.body:
![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The remote name is hardcoded to `origin`. While this is a common default, users often use other names like `upstream` for the main repository (especially when working with forks). Consider allowing the remote name to be configurable or detecting the remote associated with the current branch's upstream.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

This logic for inferring the repository context is duplicated across all PR subcommands (`list`, `view`, `create`, `merge`, `close`, `comment`). To improve maintainability and reduce boilerplate, consider refactoring this into a helper function.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Finding a PR by branch name using `owner + ":" + branch` as a filter only works for pull requests where the head branch is in the same repository as the base. For cross-repository PRs (e.g., from a fork), the head is typically `fork_owner:branch`. The fallback at line 87 might not be supported by the Gitee API or could be ambiguous. Consider if a more comprehensive search or local filtering of open PRs is needed here.

title: Add shell completion for zsh and bash

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request introduces shell completion support for both zsh and bash, including installation instructions in the README. The review feedback highlights several opportunities for improvement: adding support for pacman-style flags (e.g., -S, -R) to match the CLI definition, optimizing zsh package lookups by passing prefixes to dpkg-query, and refining argument completion for various subcommands like 'search' and 'upgrade' to ensure consistency with the tool's actual behavior.

commit.message:
Add shell completion for zsh and bash

Mirrors what apt's own completions do: apt-cache --no-generate pkgnames
for installable targets (install, show), and dpkg-query for installed
ones (remove, purge, list). Without this, the shell falls back to
filename completion for package arguments.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

Address review: pacman flags, prefix narrowing, subcommand arity

- Add pacman-style flags (-S, -R, -Q, -s, -i, -y, -u, -c, --recursive)
  to global completion in both shells, mirroring src/cli.rs.
- After -S/-R/-Q (with no subcommand), complete packages instead of
  falling back to filenames. -Ss / -Qs is treated as a free-form
  search query, so completion is suppressed there.
- zsh: pass the current prefix to dpkg-query so it returns ~6
  candidates for "libcur*" instead of all 2000+ installed packages.
- zsh: handle 'upgrade' subcommand (-f/--full) and constrain 'show'
  and 'list' to a single positional, matching the CLI definition.
- bash: complete subcommand-specific flags (e.g. upgrade -f/--full)
  before falling back to globals.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: fix(editor): typing/pasting ( now actually inserts the character

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request implements bracketed-paste mode support to prevent auto-pairing and other side effects when pasting text into the editor. It introduces a PasteState tracker to identify terminal paste markers and routes these events to bypass standard key handling. Feedback suggests improving _paste_byte to support UTF-8 characters, handle Windows-style line endings correctly to avoid double newlines, and exclude the DEL control character.

## Code Review

This pull request implements bracketed paste support, allowing the editor to handle literal text insertion by bypassing auto-pairing and auto-indentation when terminal paste markers are detected. It introduces a `PasteState` tracker to process escape sequences, updates the input handling logic to route paste content, and adds several regression tests for UTF-8 and line-ending handling. Review feedback identifies three critical issues: the 'staircase effect' caused by auto-indentation remaining active during pastes, the risk of paste content being interpreted as commands if the editor is in normal mode, and potential corruption of paste markers due to eager buffer flushing during fragmented network reads.

issue_comments.body: /gemini review

commit.message:
fix(editor): typing/pasting ( now actually inserts the character

Two compounding bugs made it impossible to enter an opening paren:

1. _auto_insert_pair (and three sibling call sites) used Pony stdlib's
   String.insert, which clones and returns a new string rather than
   mutating in place. The return was discarded, so the open/close bytes
   never landed in the buffer; the cursor advanced anyway. This also
   silently broke auto-indent on newline, :s substitute, and
   Ctrl-A/Ctrl-X number increment. Switched all four sites to
   insert_in_place.

2. Even with #1 fixed, pasting "(text" via the system clipboard would
   produce "(text)" because every byte routes through insert_key, which
   auto-pairs unconditionally. Added bracketed-paste mode (DEC ?2004):
   the terminal now wraps pasted text in \e[200~ ... \e[201~ markers,
   a new PasteState tracker in MouseInputNotify routes paste body to a
   _paste_byte path that inserts literally, and an _in_paste flag on
   Editor gates the bypass.

PasteState flushes any partial-marker hold at end of each feed() so a
typed ESC (or arrow-key prefix) isn't swallowed waiting for body bytes
that won't come — real terminals deliver markers in the same read as
the paste body, so hold-across-reads isn't worth the regression.

Tests: also fixes the test runner — Main was missing PonyTest(env, this)
so the suite had been a silent no-op. With the runner working, two
pre-existing buffer tests had wrong expectations (exclusive vs.
inclusive endpoints in delete_range); corrected to match production
semantics. Added symlinks tests/{editor,paste}.pony following the
existing tests/{buffer,syntax}.pony pattern.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(paste): keep UTF-8, drop CR in CRLF, exclude DEL

Address gemini-code-assist review on PR #5.

_paste_byte previously gated insertion on (ch >= 0x20) and (ch < 0x7F),
which silently dropped every byte of every multi-byte UTF-8 character —
accents, emoji, anything outside ASCII. Pasted Windows line endings
(\r\n) also produced double newlines because both bytes triggered
_insert_newline.

Now: \n is the only newline trigger (CR is dropped), DEL is excluded,
and bytes >= 0x80 pass through verbatim so UTF-8 sequences land intact.
Apply the same UTF-8 fix to insert_key's default branch — pre-existing
but the same root cause, and was making typed accented characters
disappear too.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -2193,6 +2200,18 @@ class Editor
       end
     end
 
+  fun ref _paste_byte(ch: U8) =>
+    """
+    Insert a single paste-content byte literally — no auto-pair, no
+    auto-skip, no Esc-exits-mode. Newlines and tabs in the paste payload
+    still produce real newlines/tabs.
+    """
+    match ch
+    | 0x0D | 0x0A => _insert_newline()
+    | 0x09 => _insert_char('\t')
+    | if (ch >= 0x20) and (ch < 0x7F) => _insert_char(ch)
+    end
review_comments.body:
![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The current implementation of `_paste_byte` has two issues:

1. **UTF-8 Support**: The guard `(ch >= 0x20) and (ch < 0x7F)` explicitly excludes all bytes from `0x80` to `0xFF`. This will cause any non-ASCII characters (like accented letters or emojis) in the pasted text to be silently dropped, even though Pony strings are UTF-8.
2. **Newline Handling**: By treating both `0x0D` (CR) and `0x0A` (LF) as newlines, pasting text with Windows-style line endings (`\r\n`) will result in double newlines in the buffer. It is safer to ignore `0x0D` and only trigger a newline on `0x0A` during a paste.

Additionally, `0x7F` (DEL) is a non-printable control character that should likely be excluded from literal insertion.

```
    match ch
    | 0x0A => _insert_newline()
    | 0x09 => _insert_char('\t')
    | if (ch >= 0x20) and (ch != 0x7F) => _insert_char(ch)
    end
```

This causes a "staircase effect" when pasting code that already contains indentation, as the editor adds its own leading whitespace on top of the pasted spaces for every new line. +    - PasteEventEnd    : the \e[201~ marker was just consumed

+  var _in_paste: Bool = false
+  embed _hold: Array[U8] = Array[U8]

+  fun ref feed(data: Array[U8] box): Array[PasteEvent] ref^ =>
+    let out = Array[PasteEvent]

+      let expected = _expected_at(_hold.size())
+      if (expected != 0) and (ch == expected) then
+        _hold.push(ch)

+          if _in_paste then
+            out.push(PasteEvent(PasteEventEnd))

+          else

+      end
+      i = i + 1
+    end

+    // \e[200~ markers as part of a single read alongside the paste body, so
+    // a leftover hold here is almost certainly a typed ESC or arrow-key

+    _flush_hold(out)
review_comments.body:
![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

Flushing the hold buffer at the end of every `feed` call breaks bracketed paste for sequences split across multiple reads. While common in local terminals, escape sequences are frequently fragmented over SSH or high-latency connections. If `\e[` arrives in one packet and `200~` in the next, this eager flush will cause the editor to interpret the partial marker as literal keystrokes, corrupting the paste. It is better to allow `_hold` to persist across calls, even if it means a single `ESC` keypress is slightly delayed until the next input byte or a timeout occurs.

commit.message:
feat: mouse click moves cursor; drag enters Visual mode

Switch SGR mouse mode from ?1000 (press only) to ?1002 (press, release,
and motion-while-held) and parse the new event types in MouseInputNotify.

Left-click moves the cursor to the clicked cell, with rx-to-cx mapping
so clicks inside a tab land on the tab character. The first drag motion
that actually moves the cursor enters Visual mode anchored at the press
point; further drag extends the selection. Click in existing Visual
cancels the selection. Status-bar and command-line clicks are ignored.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(mouse): guard tabstop=0; drop redundant drag re-render check

Address gemini-code-assist review on PR #4.

- :set tabstop=0 would crash with a divide-by-zero on the next render
  because three sites use _tab_stop as a divisor (rx % _tab_stop).
  Clamp to >=1 at the setter so all downstream call sites stay safe.

- The mouse_drag early-return condition included `(not _mouse_dragging)`,
  which forced a re-render on every motion event mid-drag — even when
  the cursor cell hadn't moved. The selection endpoint IS the cursor,
  so if it hasn't changed, nothing has changed. Drop the dragging
  clause.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

title: feat(cli): show acknowledgment when user steering hint is processed

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request implements UI feedback for user steering hints by generating and displaying acknowledgment messages. Key changes include updating the `useGeminiStream` hook to trigger these acknowledgments, adding sanitization logic in `fastAckHelper` to escape XML tags and context-breaking characters, and improving `generateSteeringAckMessage` with `AbortSignal` support for better lifecycle management. Feedback was provided regarding the sanitization regex, noting that it could be bypassed by variations in tag structure and suggesting a more robust implementation to prevent prompt injection.

## Code Review

This pull request introduces steering hint acknowledgments in the CLI UI and implements sanitization for user inputs and background outputs to mitigate prompt injection. Key changes include updating generateSteeringAckMessage to support AbortSignal propagation and adding logic to display acknowledgment messages in the message history. Feedback focuses on two critical areas: the sanitization regex for XML tags is currently too restrictive and should account for optional whitespace to prevent bypasses, and the AbortSignal used for acknowledgments in useGeminiStream may be tied to the wrong turn lifecycle, suggesting the use of queueMicrotask to capture the updated signal.

## Code Review

This pull request introduces a steering acknowledgment mechanism for the Gemini CLI, allowing the UI to provide feedback when user steering hints are processed. It includes updates to `useGeminiStream` to trigger these acknowledgments, a new `sanitizeForWrapper` utility in `fastAckHelper` to prevent prompt injection by escaping XML tags and context-breaking characters, and comprehensive unit tests for both the acknowledgment flow and the sanitization logic. My feedback highlights that the current implementation of `sanitizeForWrapper` is ineffective due to how backslashes are handled in JavaScript string literals and provides a corrected implementation.

## Code Review

This pull request introduces steering acknowledgment messages in the CLI UI and enhances prompt injection mitigation by sanitizing user input and background output. It also improves the robustness of steering acknowledgments by supporting AbortSignal for cancellation. Feedback suggests ensuring acknowledgments are only displayed if the query actually proceeds to avoid misleading feedback during quota errors, and refining the sanitization logic to remove rather than escape context-breaking characters to better align with security guidelines.

## Code Review

This pull request introduces a mechanism for generating and displaying acknowledgment messages for user steering hints, including input sanitization to prevent prompt injection and improved cancellation handling via AbortSignal. The review feedback identified a potential UI race condition where acknowledgment messages could appear out of order in the history. It is recommended to capture the acknowledgment timestamp within a microtask after the query is submitted to ensure the acknowledgment is correctly rendered after the user hint.

## Code Review

This pull request implements a mechanism for acknowledging user steering hints within the CLI. Key changes include the addition of logic in the `useGeminiStream` hook to trigger acknowledgement messages, support for `AbortSignal` in the acknowledgement generation process to handle cancellations, and new sanitization logic to mitigate prompt injection by escaping XML tags and context-breaking characters. Feedback was provided regarding the sanitization of the `]` character, suggesting it should be removed rather than escaped to more effectively prevent prompt injection.

## Code Review

This pull request adds steering acknowledgement messages to the CLI UI and implements prompt injection sanitization in the core utility functions. Key changes include updating `useGeminiStream` to handle user hints asynchronously and introducing `sanitizeForWrapper` to escape XML tags and remove context-breaking characters. Feedback suggests that the centralized sanitization logic may cause functional regressions for certain data types like logs. It is recommended to move this logic to the tool or subagent level and refine how newlines and ANSI sequences are handled to preserve structural integrity.

## Code Review

This pull request introduces steering acknowledgement messages in the CLI UI and enhances prompt injection mitigation by sanitizing inputs wrapped in XML tags. Key changes include updating `useGeminiStream` to display acknowledgements and modifying `fastAckHelper` to escape XML closing tags, remove context-breaking characters, and strip newlines. Feedback highlights that unconditional newline stripping in `sanitizeForWrapper` could break the formatting of multiline user hints, suggesting that this behavior be made optional to preserve list structures.

Addressed in 0351b52a8:

- Newlines escaped to `\n` literal inside the wrappers, per the repo prompt-injection rule. Steering paths already `normalizeSpace`, so this only changes behavior for multi-line `<background_output>` injection.
- Tests added for each of those bypass cases.

The `]` context-breaker escape was already part of this PR (line 61 in the prior version) — flagging in case the bot missed it.

/gemini review

Two new items addressed:

**Re: useGeminiStream.ts:2009 (signal captured for the wrong turn)** — fixed in e81028ad2. Wrapped the launch in `queueMicrotask` so the new turn's `AbortController` is installed before we capture the signal, and added a `turnCancelledRef.current` guard before `addItem` as belt-and-suspenders.

**Re: fastAckHelper.ts XML close-tag regex** — I think the new suggestion may be regressing the regex that already shipped in 0351b52a8. Current code (line 60) is:
```
const XML_CLOSING_TAG_RE = /<\/([^>]+)>/gi;
```
The bot's suggested `<\/(\w+)\s*>/g`:
- still uses `\w+` for the tag name, so attributes (`</user_input foo="bar">`) and inline newlines (`</user_input\n>`) inside the tag would not match;
- drops the `i` flag, so `</USER_INPUT>` would not match.

`[^>]+` already covers whitespace, attributes, embedded newlines, anything that isn't `>`. The `i` flag handles case. Tests for whitespace, attributes, and case variations are in `fastAckHelper.test.ts` (added in 0351b52a8). Let me know if I'm misreading the bot's suggestion — happy to adjust if there's a specific bypass the current regex misses.

/gemini review

Both addressed in 698fcc885:

**useGeminiStream.ts:2033** — good catch, real bug. The `queueMicrotask` block was scheduled before the `modelSwitchedFromQuotaError` early-return, so a hint that never got sent could still produce an "Understood..." line. Restructured to capture the pending ack as data inside the existing block, then dispatch it after the early-return so it only fires when `submitQuery` will actually run.

**fastAckHelper.ts:69** — partial agreement. Newlines now stripped (replaced with single spaces) inside the wrapper sanitization, per the repo rule's "remove" guidance. Kept `]` escaped (`\]`) rather than removing it, because outright removal silently changes the meaning of multi-bracket content like `[step 1] [step 2]` → `[step 1 [step 2`, which would mislead the model about its own command output. Escaping a `]` to `\]` is sufficient to neutralize bracket-format breakouts (the LLM tokenizer doesn't unescape backslashes back into structural tokens) while preserving content fidelity. Happy to revert to full removal if you'd prefer the stricter posture — flag it and I'll switch.

commit.message:
feat(core): add abort-aware sanitized steering ack helper

Extend `generateSteeringAckMessage` with an optional `signal` so callers
can cancel the request when the user aborts the active turn. Sanitize
inputs passed through `<user_input>` / `<background_output>` wrappers
to escape both XML close tags and `]` context-breakers, preventing
prompt-injection through these helpers.

Bug fixes folded in:
- short-circuit and return the fallback when the parent signal is
  already aborted (previously added a listener that never fired)
- remove the `abort` listener in a `finally` block to avoid leaking
  it across cancelled turns

Drive-by: fix the broken `'src/telemetry/llmRole.js'` import path in
the test file so the suite can be loaded under workspace tooling.

Refs #26485

feat(cli): show acknowledgment when user steering hint is processed

When the user submits a steering hint mid-turn, kick off a fast,
cancellable acknowledgment generation in parallel with the main turn
and render it as an info line in the transcript. This gives the user
an immediate, concrete signal that the hint was received and how it
was interpreted, instead of waiting silently for the model to echo
the steering itself.

The promise is fire-and-forget but properly handled:
- abort signal from the active turn is propagated; nothing is added
  to history if the turn is cancelled before the ack returns
- the timestamp is captured at submit time so the ack appears in the
  correct chronological position even if it resolves after later
  history items
- promise rejection is silently swallowed (with `AbortError` filtered)
  since the ack is non-critical UI feedback

Adds a `generateContent` response to the steering test fixture so
the request lifecycle has the response it expects.

Refs #26485

fix(core): harden wrapper sanitization against tag and newline bypasses

Address PR review feedback on the closing-tag regex:
- Match closing tags case-insensitively (`</USER_INPUT>` was previously
  passing through unsanitized).
- Allow whitespace and attributes inside the tag (`</user_input >`,
  `</background_output foo="bar">`) which many LLMs still parse as
  valid closing tags. Switch the inner capture from `\w+` to `[^>]+`.
- Escape raw newlines (LF and CRLF) to `\n` literal inside the
  wrappers, per repo prompt-injection rules. Steering paths already
  call `normalizeSpace` upstream, so this only affects multi-line
  background output going into `<background_output>`.

Tests added for whitespace-in-tag, attribute-in-tag, mixed/upper case,
and newline-escape cases.

fix(cli): tie steering ack to continuation turn's AbortController

The signal captured at the steering-hint call site belonged to the
turn that was *finishing* (the one that just ran tool calls), not the
*continuation* turn that submitQuery is about to start. submitQuery
installs a new AbortController synchronously inside its body, so the
signal we had already captured was orphaned: a Ctrl+C during the
continuation aborts the new controller while our captured signal
stays live, leaving the ack to post a "ghost" history item after the
user thought they cancelled.

Defer the signal capture and ack launch into a microtask so the new
controller is in place first. Also guard the `addItem` call on
`turnCancelledRef.current` as a belt-and-suspenders check against the
acknowledgment outliving cancellation.

fix: skip steering ack on quota-error early-return; strip newlines

Two PR review fixes:

useGeminiStream.ts — the queueMicrotask launch sat *before* the
modelSwitchedFromQuotaError early-return, so a hint that never
actually got sent to the model could still produce an "Understood..."
acknowledgment in the transcript. Capture the pending ack as data,
then dispatch it after the early-return.

fastAckHelper.ts — replace raw newlines with single spaces inside
wrapper sanitization rather than escaping them. The repo prompt-
injection rules favor removal over escaping for newlines and other
structural breakers; spaces preserve content separation while
removing the line-break structure that could escape the wrapper.
The `]` context-breaker stays escaped (`\]`) since outright removal
would silently change the meaning of multi-bracket content like
`[step 1] [step 2]` and the escape is sufficient to neutralize
bracket-based prompt-format breakouts.

fix(cli): capture steering ack timestamp inside the microtask

The previous spot for `Date.now()` ran before submitQuery had assigned
its own message timestamp, so the ack risked sorting *above* the hint
it was acknowledging in a timestamp-ordered history. Move the capture
inside the queueMicrotask, which runs after submitQuery's synchronous
prefix; the ack now lands strictly after the hint while still ahead
of any model response chunks.

fix(core): remove context-breaking ] characters instead of escaping

Per repository sanitization rules, context-breaking characters like ']'
should be removed rather than escaped when sanitizing untrusted data
before injection into model prompts. Escaping is not guaranteed to
neutralize structural tokens depending on tokenizer behavior.

Update tests to reflect the new removal semantics.
fix(core): address review comments on steering hint sanitization

   'Do not cancel/skip tasks unless the user explicitly cancels them. ' +

- * Wraps user input in XML-like tags to mitigate prompt injection.

review_comments.body:
![security-high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/security-high-priority.svg) ![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The regular expression `XML_TAG_REPLACE_RE` used for sanitizing untrusted input is incomplete and can be bypassed. It only matches closing XML tags that consist of word characters and are immediately followed by a `>`. An attacker can bypass this by including whitespace or attributes within the tag (e.g., `</user_input >`), which many LLMs will still interpret as a valid closing tag. This allows for prompt injection. Additionally, repository rules require that data from LLM-driven tools be sanitized by removing newlines and context-breaking characters (like ']') before injection into system prompts. The regex should be case-insensitive and robust against variations in tag structure to ensure all variations of closing tags are caught.

```suggestion

commit.message:
fix(tui): bind home/end to line start/end in input

Previously home/end (sent by fn+left/right on Apple keyboards and the
Home/End keys on PC layouts) were claimed by both messages_first/last
and input_buffer_home/end, so pressing them in the input jumped through
the message history instead of the current line. Move home/end onto

and shift the buffer-jump bindings to ctrl+home/ctrl+end (and
ctrl+shift+home/end for selection). Drop the home/end aliases from the
message-navigation bindings; ctrl+g and ctrl+alt+g still work for those.

longer accurate now that those aliases were removed from messages_first
and messages_last in the previous commit.

title: Parallelize chunk generation across rayon worker pool

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request refactors the chunk generation system to be asynchronous by offloading terrain generation to a Rayon worker pool. It introduces new state flags to manage the lifecycle of chunks during generation and post-processing. While the architectural change is sound, the implementation introduces potential data races by using plain booleans for cross-thread signaling, and the new spiral generation and completion scanning logic may introduce performance bottlenecks due to O(N^2) operations in the main loop.

commit.message:
perf: parallelize chunk generation across rayon worker pool

Chunk::generate() previously ran on the render thread, throttled to 8 chunks
per frame in normal mode and 64 in initial loading. With VIEW_DISTANCE=35
that's a 5,041-chunk pool sampled serially on a single core while the rest
of the CPU sat idle.

This change dispatches generation onto the existing rayon pool the same way
meshing already was. The spiral allocates a stub Chunk, marks it
generation_in_progress, hands a *mut Chunk to a worker, and the worker calls
generate() then flips the flag. A new post-process pass on the main thread
picks up finished chunks, runs apply_edits_to_chunk, and dirties self +
neighbors for meshing.

Other changes that fall out:

- Slot recycling now refuses to drop a chunk while its worker is in flight.
- Per-frame dispatch caps removed: rayon's queue handles back-pressure, and
  capping at 16 just left cores idle on first load.
- A MAX_UPLOADS_PER_FRAME cap is added on GPU upload (the only main-thread
  serialization point left), but skipped during the initial load so meshes
  drain as fast as the GPU accepts them.
- is_loading now waits for generation_in_flight, meshing_in_flight, and
  dirty_count to all reach zero before flipping — otherwise the player
  spawns into an empty sky while meshes are still building.
- chunks_generated_count ticks per uploaded chunk (not per generated chunk)
  so the loading bar tracks the slow path, not the fast one.
- Chunk::new defaults dirty: false now that the post-process step is
  responsible for flipping the bit and updating World::dirty_count.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

perf: gate per-frame spiral on movement or pending-skip

Running the spiral every frame meant ~4,900 try_dispatch_generate calls per
frame, each touching a separate 192KB Box<Chunk> for the c.x == cx check.
That cache pressure dropped steady-state framerate from 144 to ~50.

Re-add the position gate, but track a spiral_pending flag set by
try_dispatch_generate when it has to skip a busy slot — so the spiral
self-recovers on the next frame instead of leaving the slot stale forever
(the original concern that motivated removing the gate).

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -108,6 +108,12 @@ pub struct Chunk {
     pub pending_mesh_water: Option<MeshData>,
     pub dirty: bool,
     pub meshing_in_progress: bool,
+    // True while a rayon worker is running `generate()` against this chunk's blocks.
+    // Used to guard slot recycling and to skip meshing dispatch until generation finishes.
+    pub generation_in_progress: bool,
review_comments.body:
![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The `generation_in_progress` flag is accessed concurrently by the main thread and a Rayon worker thread. Using a plain `bool` for cross-thread signaling without synchronization is Undefined Behavior (UB) in Rust. It should be changed to an `AtomicBool` with appropriate memory ordering (e.g., `Release` on write, `Acquire` on load) to ensure that the terrain generation results are fully visible to the main thread before the flag is seen as `false`.

```suggestion
pub generation_in_progress: std::sync::atomic::AtomicBool,
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Scanning the entire chunk pool every frame to find finished generations is inefficient. As the view distance increases, this $O(N^2)$ scan becomes more expensive. Consider using a queue or a bitset to track only the chunks that are currently in flight, allowing you to check only those that could potentially have finished.

title: fix: prevent status bar from wrapping when narrower than its content

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request refactors the status bar rendering logic in src/editor.pony to ensure the output fits exactly within the terminal width, preventing unwanted line wrapping. Feedback highlights a potential issue with UTF-8 safety when truncating strings by byte count, which could lead to invalid sequences, and suggests a refactored approach to reduce code duplication and handle character boundaries correctly.

## Code Review

This pull request refactors the status bar rendering in `src/editor.pony` to ensure the output fits within the terminal's column width. It introduces logic to truncate the left side at UTF-8 boundaries and a `_pad` helper function. Feedback highlights that the implementation uses byte counts instead of visual column widths, which will cause layout issues when handling multi-byte characters.

issue_comments.body: /gemini review

commit.message:
fix: prevent status bar from wrapping when narrower than its content

When `left + right` exceeds `_cols`, the bar was emitted in full and
relied on the terminal to wrap, which scrolled the screen up by one
row and shifted the cursor onto the line below the intended target.
Now fit-or-truncate so the bar always occupies exactly `_cols` cells.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(status-bar): truncate at UTF-8 codepoint boundary; dedupe padding

Address gemini-code-assist review on PR #3.

substring(0, _cols) was a byte-count cut, not a codepoint cut. Truncating
mid-codepoint emits an incomplete UTF-8 sequence, which terminals render
as a replacement glyph or, worse, may corrupt subsequent output. Now
back up the limit until the byte at that position is no longer a
continuation byte (10xxxxxx), so the cut lands on a real codepoint
boundary.

Also extracted the repeated padding-with-spaces loop into a small _pad
helper — three identical loops collapsed to one.

Note: the byte-count == cell-count assumption is preserved (a 2-byte é
still counts as 2 cells here). That's a separate problem affecting the
whole renderer; addressing it here would be out of scope.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -3538,19 +3538,30 @@ class Editor
     right.append((_cx + 1).string())
     right.append(" ")
 
-    let padding = if _cols > (left.size() + right.size()) then
-      _cols - left.size() - right.size()
+    // Fit `left` + padding + `right` into exactly `_cols` cells. If the two
+    // sides together exceed `_cols`, drop the right side first, then truncate
+    // left. Overflowing past `_cols` would wrap to a second terminal row,
+    // which scrolls the screen and shifts the cursor off the target line.
+    if (left.size() + right.size()) <= _cols then
+      out.append(left)
+      var p: USize = 0
+      let padding = _cols - left.size() - right.size()
+      while p < padding do
+        out.push(' ')
+        p = p + 1
+      end
+      out.append(right)
+    elseif left.size() <= _cols then
+      out.append(left)
+      var p: USize = 0
+      let padding = _cols - left.size()
+      while p < padding do
+        out.push(' ')
+        p = p + 1
+      end
     else
-      0
-    end
-
-    out.append(left)
-    var p: USize = 0
-    while p < padding do
-      out.push(' ')
-      p = p + 1
+      out.append(left.substring(0, _cols.isize()))
     end
review_comments.body:
![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The current implementation has two issues:
1. **UTF-8 Safety**: Truncating `left` by byte count (`substring(0, _cols)`) can split a multi-byte UTF-8 character, resulting in invalid sequences being sent to the terminal. This can cause rendering artifacts.
2. **Code Duplication**: The padding logic is repeated across branches.

This refactored version simplifies the logic, avoids invalid UTF-8 truncation by checking character boundaries, and ensures the status bar always fills exactly `_cols` cells (assuming 1 byte = 1 cell, which is consistent with the rest of the editor's logic).

```
    let l_sz = left.size()
    let r_sz = right.size()

    if (l_sz + r_sz) <= _cols then
      out.append(left)
      var p = _cols - l_sz - r_sz
      while p > 0 do
        out.push(' ')
        p = p - 1
      end
      out.append(right)
    elseif l_sz <= _cols then
      out.append(left)
      var p = _cols - l_sz
      while p > 0 do
        out.push(' ')
        p = p - 1
      end
    else
      var limit = _cols
      try
        // Ensure we don't truncate in the middle of a UTF-8 character
        while (limit > 0) and ((left(limit)? and 0xC0) == 0x80) do
          limit = limit - 1
        end
      end
      left.truncate(limit)
      out.append(left)
      var p = _cols - limit
      while p > 0 do
        out.push(' ')
        p = p - 1
      end
    end
```

review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -3538,20 +3538,40 @@ class Editor
     right.append((_cx + 1).string())
     right.append(" ")
 
-    let padding = if _cols > (left.size() + right.size()) then
-      _cols - left.size() - right.size()
+    // Fit `left` + padding + `right` into exactly `_cols` cells. If the two
+    // sides together exceed `_cols`, drop the right side first, then truncate
+    // left. Overflowing past `_cols` would wrap to a second terminal row,
+    // which scrolls the screen and shifts the cursor off the target line.
+    let l_sz = left.size()
+    let r_sz = right.size()
review_comments.body:
![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The logic compares `l_sz` (the number of bytes in the string) directly against `_cols` (the number of terminal columns). This assumes a 1:1 mapping between bytes and columns, which is incorrect for multi-byte UTF-8 characters (e.g., a 3-byte '€' usually occupies only 1 column). 

This discrepancy leads to two issues:
1. **Premature Truncation**: The status bar will be truncated even if the visual width of the text would have fit within the available columns.
2. **Incorrect Padding**: The `_pad` function calculates the number of spaces based on byte counts (`_cols - l_sz` or `_cols - limit`). If the text contains multi-byte characters, the resulting status bar will be visually shorter than the terminal width, causing the inverse-video background to end prematurely.

While calculating true Unicode display width is non-trivial, using byte counts as a proxy for column width is a known limitation here that will affect non-ASCII filenames or paths.

title: fix: ensure closing fence in WrapCodeBlock is on its own line

commit.message:
fix: ensure closing fence in `WrapCodeBlock` is on its own line

If the input does not end with a newline, the closing ``` ends up on the
same line as the last content line, so Markdown renderers never close the
code block. Append a newline only when missing to avoid introducing a

title: chore(deps): replace image and rayon with smaller alternatives

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request removes the image and rayon dependencies, replacing them with the png crate for texture loading and std::thread::spawn for chunk meshing. However, the transition to std::thread::spawn introduces significant risks, including performance overhead from spawning OS threads, memory unsoundness due to raw pointer usage in a 'static context, and potential data races. Additionally, the new PNG decoding implementation is fragile as it lacks support for grayscale formats and 16-bit images, which may lead to panics or rendering artifacts.

commit.message: chore(deps): replace image and rayon with smaller alternatives

review_comments.body:
![security-critical](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/security-critical.svg) ![critical](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/critical.svg)

Replacing `rayon::spawn` with `std::thread::spawn` in this context introduces critical performance and safety concerns:

1. **Performance Regression**: `std::thread::spawn` creates a new OS thread every time it is called. Spawning up to 16 threads per frame (as limited by `dispatches_this_frame`) can lead to significant overhead and resource exhaustion. `rayon` uses a work-stealing thread pool which is much more efficient for these types of short-lived tasks.
2. **Memory Unsoundness**: The closure uses raw pointers (`world_ptr`, `chunk_ptr`) to bypass Rust's lifetime checks. Since `std::thread::spawn` requires a `'static` lifetime, there is no guarantee that the `World` or `Chunk` objects will remain valid while the background thread is running, leading to potential use-after-free vulnerabilities.
3. **Data Races**: The background thread writes to `c.pending_mesh_opaque` (and other fields) while the main thread reads them in the same `update` loop. Without synchronization (like a `Mutex` or `Atomic`), this is a data race and undefined behavior.

If the goal is to remove the `rayon` dependency, consider using a dedicated background worker thread with a `std::sync::mpsc` channel to safely return meshing results to the main thread.

title: feat(substitute): add regex support to :s/ command

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request replaces literal string matching in the substitute command with a custom regex engine supporting quantifiers, character classes, and anchors. Review feedback identifies a bug where the ^ anchor is incorrectly handled during global replacements, potentially matching at any position. Additionally, the recursive matching strategy poses a stack overflow risk, and character class escapes are currently not supported within bracketed sets.

## Code Review

This pull request replaces literal string matching with a custom regex engine for the editor's substitute command, supporting common patterns, quantifiers, and character classes. Feedback highlights a critical issue where `tests/regex.pony` contains an invalid path string instead of source code, which will cause compilation failures. Other recommendations focus on performance optimizations, such as pre-allocating capacities for strings and arrays and using constants for frequently used character class ranges to reduce garbage collection overhead.

issue_comments.body: /gemini review

commit.message:
feat(substitute): add regex support to :s/ command

Implements a pure-Pony backtracking regex engine in src/regex.pony
and wires it into the :s/, :%s/, and :.,$s/ commands. Previously
substitution used literal string matching only.

Supports: . ^ $ * + ? [abc] [a-z] [^...] \d\D\w\W\s\S\n\t\r\\
and & / \& in replacement text. No capture groups or alternation.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

fix(regex): anchor must respect start position; support \d \w \s in classes

Address gemini-code-assist review on PR #1.

Three fixes to the regex engine, plus tests:

- ^ anchor: find() ignored the caller's start argument when _anchored
  was true, so :s/^a/b/g on "aa" matched at position 0, then matched
  AGAIN at position 1 because _match_from was happily re-checking from
  whatever position. ^ binds to literal start of input only — when start
  > 0, the find must fail.

- [\d] / [\w] / [\s]: character class escapes weren't handled inside
  [...] so [\d] matched the literal letter 'd'. Added expansion for the
  positive forms (\D \W \S inside a class would need a complement we
  can't express as a simple range list, so they fall through to literal
  — still better than silent corruption since gemini's review flagged
  the positive forms specifically).

- Latent qmin bug surfaced by the new tests: _match_from greedily
  consumed up to qmax, then immediately tried to match the rest. If
  greedy reached count < qmin (e.g. + with no atom matches), the engine
  still returned success with count=0 because the qmin check only
  guarded the backtrack-fail path. So /a+/ would zero-match anywhere.
  Added an explicit count < qmin failure before backtracking.

Test infrastructure: also includes the Main = PonyTest(env, this) fix
and the inclusive-endpoint corrections to delete_range tests; both are
also in PR #5 and will merge cleanly either way.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 <noreply@anthropic.com>

review_comments.diff_hunk:
@@ -0,0 +1,272 @@
+// Minimal regex engine for Colt's :s/ command.
+// Supports: literals, . ^ $ * + ? [abc] [^abc] [a-z]
+// Escapes: \d \D \w \W \s \S \n \t \r \\ \. \* \+ \? \[ \] \^ \$
+// Greedy quantifiers, no capture groups.
+
+class val _ReAtom
+  // kind: 0=literal, 1=any, 2=class, 3=negclass, 5=end-anchor
+  let kind: U8
+  let byte: U8
+  let ranges: Array[(U8, U8)] val
+  let qmin: USize
+  let qmax: USize
+
+  new val create(k: U8, b: U8, r: Array[(U8, U8)] val,
+    qn: USize, qx: USize) =>
+    kind = k
+    byte = b
+    ranges = r
+    qmin = qn
+    qmax = qx
+
+class val Regex
+  let _atoms: Array[_ReAtom] val
+  let _anchored: Bool
+
+  new val create(atoms: Array[_ReAtom] val, anchored: Bool) =>
+    _atoms = atoms
+    _anchored = anchored
+
+  fun find(s: String box, start: USize): (USize, USize) ? =>
+    """Find first match at or after start. Returns (match_start, match_end)."""
+    var pos = start
+    let n = s.size()
+    if _anchored then
+      let r = _match_from(0, s, pos)
+      if r._1 then return (pos, r._2) end
+      error
+    end
review_comments.body:
![high](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/high-priority.svg)

The `^` anchor implementation is incorrect for global replacements. Currently, if `_anchored` is true, the engine attempts to match the pattern at the current `pos`. However, `^` should only match at the very beginning of the string (index 0). In a global replacement like `:s/^a/b/g` on the string `"aa"`, the first match at index 0 will succeed, but the second call to `find` with `pos > 0` will also succeed because the `^` character was stripped during compilation and `_anchored` just forces a match at the current position. This leads to `^` matching every character position if the rest of the pattern matches.

```
    if _anchored then
      if pos > 0 then error end
      let r = _match_from(0, s, 0)
      if r._1 then return (0, r._2) end
      error
    end
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

The backtracking implementation uses recursion, which can lead to a stack overflow for very long strings or highly complex regular expressions (ReDoS). While this is a minimal engine, consider using an iterative approach with an explicit stack if performance or robustness against malicious patterns is a concern.

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Character class escapes like `\d`, `\w`, or `\s` are not supported inside `[...]` blocks. They will be treated as literal characters (e.g., `[\d]` matches a literal 'd'). While the PR description only lists simple classes, supporting standard escapes inside classes is common practice.

This file contains a relative path string instead of valid Pony source code. If this was intended to be a symbolic link, it is not correctly represented in the repository as a symlink object. If it is a regular file, it will cause a compilation error when `ponyc` attempts to build the test package, as `..` is not a valid Pony token at the start of a file.

review_comments.body:
![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

An empty `Array[(U8, U8)] val` is allocated for every atom processed in the pattern, even for literal characters that do not use ranges. This creates unnecessary garbage for the GC. Consider defining a single `empty_ranges` constant at the primitive level and reusing it.

```
      var rs: Array[(U8, U8)] val = _empty_ranges

  fun _empty_ranges(): Array[(U8, U8)] val =>
    recover val Array[(U8, U8)] end
```

![medium](https://www.gstatic.com/codereviewagent/medium-priority.svg)

Shorthand escape sequences like `\d`, `\w`, and `\s` allocate a new array for their ranges every time they are encountered in a pattern. Since these ranges are constant, they should be defined as `val` constants to avoid repeated allocations.

title: feat(runtime): pure-Rust runtime crate; port point.c

reviews.body:
## Code Review

This pull request initiates the incremental port of the tree-sitter C runtime to Rust, starting with the point-related logic. It introduces a new `tree-sitter-runtime` crate, re-implements point manipulation functions with C ABI compatibility, and updates the CMake build system to link this Rust static library. The review feedback highlights critical issues in the CMake configuration regarding cross-platform support, build type handling, and cross-compilation. Additionally, it is recommended to use wrapping arithmetic in the Rust implementation to ensure parity with C's overflow behavior and prevent runtime panics in debug builds.

commit.message:
feat(runtime): introduce pure-Rust runtime crate; port point.c

Adds crates/runtime as a staticlib-exposed Rust crate that will progressively
replace the C runtime under lib/src/ while preserving the C ABI.

First ported module is point.c: ts_point_edit is now implemented in Rust and
linked into libtree-sitter via CMake (point.c is excluded from the C source
glob; cargo is invoked via add_custom_command and the resulting
libtree_sitter_runtime.a is linked into the tree-sitter target).

Verified:
  - cargo test -p tree-sitter-runtime (7 unit tests)
  - cmake --build builds libtree-sitter.so cleanly
  - nm shows ts_point_edit exported from the Rust staticlib
  - a C driver linked against libtree-sitter.so produces identical results
    to the prior C implementation for before/inside/after edit cases

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

review(runtime): address gemini review on PR #1

- point.rs: use wrapping_add for uint32_t arithmetic in point_add and
  ts_point_edit to match C's defined overflow semantics and avoid
  panics in debug builds.
- CMakeLists.txt: respect CMAKE_BUILD_TYPE (Debug -> target/debug,
  otherwise target/release), use CMAKE_STATIC_LIBRARY_{PREFIX,SUFFIX}
  for Windows/MSVC portability, and pass --target to cargo when
  CMAKE_CROSSCOMPILING with a known CMAKE_C_COMPILER_TARGET so the
  staticlib lands at target/<triple>/<profile>/.

Verified:
  - cargo test -p tree-sitter-runtime (7 passing)
  - cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Release builds from target/release
  - cmake -DCMAKE_BUILD_TYPE=Debug builds from target/debug

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

issue_comments.body: Hi @euxaristia, what's the goal of this PR? Why is this version preferable to what's there? Is it intended as a replacement?

@bfops rewrites the C++ binding layer in Rust. The C++ side has integration tests (type-isolation, client-comparison) but zero unit tests on the internal logic, so edge cases/errors aren't covered. This adds 96 unit tests at 100% coverage, uses shared Rust types directly, and keeps everything in one toolchain.

Yes, intended as a replacement, but this PR only adds the crate — nothing removed. Follow-up would swap it in if the team's on board.

commit.message:
feat(bindings-cpp-ffi): add Rust replacement for C++ bindings

Rewrite the core C++ type registration and FFI dispatch layer from
crates/bindings-cpp in Rust. The new crate provides:

- ModuleTypeRegistration: full SATS type registration with circular
  reference detection, error module generation, and BSATN serialization
- FFI dispatch: __describe_module__, __call_reducer__, __call_view__,
  __call_view_anon__, __call_procedure__ with proper Identity/ConnectionId
  reconstruction and BytesSource/BytesSink I/O
- Preinit functions: __preinit__01_clear_global_state and
  __preinit__99_validate_types matching the C++ originals

Replaces ~1,414 lines of C++ with ~1,139 lines of Rust (19% reduction)
plus 96 unit tests (100% coverage). Clippy-clean and fmt-clean.

fix(bindings-cpp-ffi): track read cursor in test host stubs

- Store (data, cursor) instead of raw Vec<u8>
- bytes_source_read: copy from cursor position, advance cursor
- bytes_source_read: return 0 while data remains, -1 when exhausted
- bytes_source_read: always write *len (0 when exhausted) to avoid
  reading uninitialized buffer capacity as valid bytes
- bytes_source_remaining_length: report remaining bytes, not total

review_comments.body:
**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Define byte handle types as u32 to match host ABI**

`BytesSink` and `BytesSource` are declared as `u64`, but the host ABI in this repository passes byte-source/sink IDs as `u32` (for example, `CALL_REDUCER_SIG` expects `i32` handle arguments and the host shims accept `u32` IDs). This width mismatch changes the FFI contract for imported/exported functions and can cause module signature mismatch or incorrect handle decoding when `__call_*` and byte source/sink functions are invoked.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

**<sub><sub>![P1 Badge](https://img.shields.io/badge/P1-orange?style=flat)</sub></sub>  Advance buffer length only after successful source reads**

`buf.set_len(buf.len() + buf_len)` is executed before validating `ret` from `bytes_source_read`. If the host returns an error code (for example `NO_SUCH_BYTES`) without updating `buf_len`, this marks uninitialized spare capacity as initialized bytes and then continues with corrupted data/error handling. That is memory-unsafe and can surface as garbage reducer/view/procedure arguments; update the length only on successful/exhausted reads where bytes were actually written.

Useful? React with 👍 / 👎.

commit.message:
feat: add bun runtime support

Add opt-in Bun runtime support for running Qwen Code with Bun.

Changes:
- Detect Bun at runtime and disable node-pty (avoids EAGAIN errors under Bun's
  incomplete native-addon support) via shouldDefaultToNodePty()
- Use bun for build/start scripts (scripts/build.js, scripts/start.js,
  scripts/check-build-status.js)
- Use bun for bundle/prepare npm scripts (package.json)
- Add ink@6.2.3 patch to silently handle react-devtools-core incompatibility
  with Bun
- Add bun.lock for deterministic Bun installs
- Update husky pre-commit to use bun
- Update VSCode companion NOTICES.txt with Bun-added deps

Node.js users are unaffected by the shebang, package metadata, or published
dependency set; the CLI binary still reports #!/usr/bin/env node.

Tested on Linux with Bun 1.3.10.

Co-authored-by: Qwen-Coder <qwen-coder@alibabacloud.com>

fix: address PR review comments - make Bun opt-in via QWEN_RUNTIME env var, regenerate bun.lock, restore NOTICES.txt, fix ink patch

- Revert package.json scripts (bundle, prepare) to use npm by default
- Make build.js and start.js use QWEN_RUNTIME=bun env var for opt-in Bun support
- Regenerate bun.lock from clean manifests (fixes stale versions)
- Restore NOTICES.txt to upstream (Bun regeneration caused license text issues)
- Make ink patch more targeted (preserve ERR_MODULE_NOT_FOUND warning, only suppress in Bun)
- Revert .husky/pre-commit and check-build-status.js messages to npm

commit.message:
fix: swallow resize() error when conpty has already exited

Silently ignore resize calls after the PTY process has exited instead
of throwing 'Cannot resize a pty that has already exited'. This prevents
crashes on Windows with Node.js v22 when a resize is attempted after
the process has exited.

Fixes #827

fix: swallow EBADF on resize() when PTY fd is already closed

pty.resize() throws an error with message 'ioctl(2) failed, EBADF' when
the PTY file descriptor has already been closed (e.g. the process exited
and the fd was destroyed before a resize event fired). Catch EBADF by
both error code and message and silently return instead of crashing.
